verses.text: David therefore departed thence, and escaped to the cave Adullam: and when his brethren and all his father's house heard it, they went down thither to him. And every one that was in distress, and every one that was in debt, and every one that was discontented, gathered themselves unto him; and he became a captain over them: and there were with him about four hundred men. Guidance: David comes east out of Philistine territory and finds shelter in the cave of Adullam — a large limestone cave system in the Judean foothills, about ten miles west of Bethlehem. The first arrivals are his own brothers and his father's family (probably because Saul, having now confirmed David is in open revolt, would target the family next; they come to David for safety, not the other way around). Then strangers begin to drift in. The narrator lists them with precise sociology: 'in distress' (those under pressure), 'in debt' (creditors after them, possibly with insolvency contracts that meant losing children to bond-servitude), 'discontented' (literally 'bitter of soul' — the chronically angry at how things are). Four hundred of them. This is the beginning of David's army. Notice what kind of army it is. Not professional soldiers, not court appointees, not the picked elite of Israel — the *failed and the bitter*, society's edge. God's chosen king is being formed at the head of an unlikely band of people the kingdom had no use for. The pattern will recur in scripture: God's most powerful instruments are often assembled from the rejected. Adullam is a foretaste of grace; the cave that will produce a kingdom is open to the in-debt and the discontented, because the one inside is a man after God's own heart who knows what it is to be hunted.

And David went thence to Mizpeh of Moab: and he said unto the king of Moab, Let my father and my mother, I pray thee, come forth, and be with you, till I know what God will do for me. And he brought them before the king of Moab: and they dwelt with him all the while that David was in the hold. Guidance: David moves his parents east across the Jordan into Moab — the homeland of his great-grandmother Ruth (Ruth 4:17 — Jesse is the son of Obed, son of Ruth and Boaz). The Moabite connection probably explains why the king of Moab is willing to host David's parents; David is in part Moabite blood, and the family tie carries weight. Jesse and his unnamed mother are elderly (Jesse was already an old man by 17:12); David places them in safety where Saul cannot reach. The phrase 'till I know what God will do for me' is one of the chapter's gentlest sentences. David does not pretend to know how the wilderness years will end. He commits the future to God's hand and meanwhile arranges practical care for his vulnerable parents. Faith and arrangement are not opposites — David trusts God for what God will do and shelters his parents while waiting to find out. Care for elderly parents in a season of personal crisis is, very often, the most concrete shape of obedience available.

And the prophet Gad said unto David, Abide not in the hold; depart, and get thee into the land of Judah. Then David departed, and came into the forest of Hareth. Guidance: A new character enters: 'the prophet Gad.' Gad will be David's resident prophet for the rest of his life (mentioned again in 2 Sam 24, where he brings David the LORD's word about the census disaster). Where Samuel anointed David and disappeared to Ramah, Gad now begins the prophetic accompaniment of David's daily moves. Gad's first word is operational: 'Abide not in the hold; depart, and get thee into the land of Judah.' The hold (probably either Mizpeh of Moab or the original Adullam cave) is too remote; Judah, David's home tribe, is the natural recruiting ground and political base for the man God is making king. David obeys instantly — moves to 'the forest of Hareth' (location unknown, somewhere in the Judean hill country). Notice the cooperation: David has a band of 400, a sympathetic Moab connection, parents safely lodged — and he still obeys the prophet's word to move out. Faith does not exempt one from prophetic guidance; if anything, it makes one more responsive to it. God provides prophets to fugitive saints so that the fugitive's instincts do not become the only navigation system.

When Saul heard that David was discovered, and the men that were with him, (now Saul abode in Gibeah under a tree in Ramah, having his spear in his hand, and all his servants were standing about him;) Then Saul said unto his servants that stood about him, Hear now, ye Benjamites; will the son of Jesse give every one of you fields and vineyards, and make you all captains of thousands, and captains of hundreds; That all of you have conspired against me, and there is none that sheweth me that my son hath made a league with the son of Jesse, and there is none of you that is sorry for me, or sheweth unto me that my son hath stirred up my servant against me, to lie in wait, as at this day? Guidance: The camera cuts to Saul, posed under a tamarisk tree at Gibeah, spear in hand (the spear is now a constant; he has not put it down since 19:9), servants standing around him. The visual is paranoia in tableau. Then comes one of the saddest speeches in the Old Testament. Saul's argument is purely political: 'will the son of Jesse give every one of you fields and vineyards, and make you all captains?' He is asking his Benjamite kinsmen whether David could possibly outbid him for their loyalty. (Why Benjamites? Because Saul, of the tribe of Benjamin, has been dispensing the spoils of his reign disproportionately to his own tribe — David, of Judah, would presumably favor the southerners.) The speech is also self-pitying: 'none of you have any compassion on me… my son has stirred up my servant against me.' Saul is now sure his own son is the head of a conspiracy. The king who hides behind a spear is also the king who hears every silence as treason. Mistrust is the prison of the unrepentant; once you've imagined a conspiracy, every absence of news confirms it.

Then answered Doeg the Edomite, which was set over the servants of Saul, and said, I saw the son of Jesse coming to Nob, to Ahimelech the son of Ahitub. And he enquired of the LORD for him, and gave him victuals, and gave him the sword of Goliath the Philistine. Guidance: Doeg, the Edomite from chapter 21, steps forward. The narrator's title for him here is grimmer than 21:7 — 'set over the servants of Saul,' indicating he has been promoted, perhaps for this very loyalty. Doeg testifies. His testimony is technically accurate — Ahimelech did enquire of the LORD for David, did give him bread, did give him Goliath's sword — but the omissions are murderous. Doeg does not mention that David lied to Ahimelech with a story of a secret royal mission. Doeg does not mention that Ahimelech had no idea David was a fugitive. The testimony is the truth with the exculpating context surgically removed. This is the chapter's portrait of the most dangerous kind of witness: one whose facts are correct and whose framing is lethal. Psalm 52, written about this very Doeg, names what he is: 'thy tongue deviseth mischiefs; like a sharp razor, working deceitfully… thou lovest evil more than good; and lying rather than to speak righteousness.' Half-truths in the wrong ears can be more lethal than outright lies.

Then answered Doeg the Edomite, which was set over the servants of Saul, and said, I saw the son of Jesse coming to Nob, to Ahimelech the son of Ahitub. And he enquired of the LORD for him, and gave him victuals, and gave him the sword of Goliath the Philistine. Guidance: Doeg, the Edomite from chapter 21, steps forward. Then the king sent to call Ahimelech the priest, the son of Ahitub, and all his father's house, the priests that were in Nob: and they came all of them to the king. And Saul said, Hear now, thou son of Ahitub. And he answered, Here I am, my lord. And Saul said unto him, Why have ye conspired against me, thou and the son of Jesse, in that thou hast given him bread, and a sword, and hast enquired of God for him, that he should rise against me, to lie in wait, as at this day? Guidance: Saul summons the entire priestly community at Nob — not just Ahimelech but his whole extended family of priests. He intends to make this hearing a mass trial. Saul addresses Ahimelech not by name but by patronymic: 'thou son of Ahitub.' Ahimelech's answer — 'here I am, my lord' (hinneni, the word of a faithful servant at God's call) — is the language of a man who still believes he is in a covenant kingdom whose king is the LORD's anointed. Saul then makes the accusation: conspiracy, three counts — bread, sword, oracle. Three things Doeg said Ahimelech did. The accusation is the kind made by an absolute monarch whose paranoia has now generalized to 'anyone who helped him is plotting against me.' There is no room in the proceeding for the question of what Ahimelech *believed* he was doing. Saul has already decided. The judicial form is being used as a vehicle for vengeance dressed as justice.

Then the king sent to call Ahimelech the priest, the son of Ahitub, and all his father's house, the priests that were in Nob: and they came all of them to the king. Then Ahimelech answered the king, and said, And who is so faithful among all thy servants as David, which is the king's son in law, and goeth at thy bidding, and is honourable in thine house? Did I then begin to enquire of God for him? be it far from me: let not the king impute any thing unto his servant, nor to all the house of my father: for thy servant knew nothing of all this, less or more. Guidance: Ahimelech's defense is dignified and entirely honest. Three points. (1) David has been the most faithful of all your servants — your son-in-law, your courier, honored in your household. Helping him was not an act against you; it was an act for you. (2) Was that the first time I enquired of the LORD for David? Implication: I have done this many times before in the course of his ordinary court duties, without controversy. (3) Most importantly: 'thy servant knew nothing of all this, less or more.' Ahimelech had no idea David was fleeing the king. He acted in good faith on what he was told. He cannot defend the framing Doeg gave; he can only deny the inner mental state Saul is imputing. Note that Ahimelech does not name David's lie. He does not say, 'David lied to me, so I am not responsible.' He stays in the kingdom's frame and asks Saul to believe what he knew. There is a kind of integrity that does not protect itself by shifting blame to a person who is not in the room to defend himself. Ahimelech could have saved his own life by exposing David; he chooses not to. He will pay for the choice within minutes.

And the king said, Thou shalt surely die, Ahimelech, thou, and all thy father's house. And the king said unto the footmen that stood about him, Turn, and slay the priests of the LORD; because their hand also is with David, and because they knew when he fled, and did not shew it to me. But the servants of the king would not put forth their hand to fall upon the priests of the LORD. Guidance: Saul ignores the defense. 'Thou shalt surely die, Ahimelech, thou, and all thy father's house.' Capital sentence on the entire extended family of priests — sentence imposed without hearing them, without proof, without the witness rule of Deut 19:15. Then the order to execute: 'Turn, and slay the priests of the LORD.' The phrase 'priests of the LORD' is the narrator's editorial commentary on the depth of the sin; even Saul's own command uses ordinary language ('priests'), but the text reminds us what we are watching — the anointed king ordering the murder of God's anointed servants. Then the breakthrough sentence: 'But the servants of the king would not put forth their hand to fall upon the priests of the LORD.' Saul's own soldiers refuse the order. They will fight enemies, they will pursue David, but they draw the line at slaughtering unarmed clergy. This is one of the noblest small moments in 1 Samuel. There are limits to what subordinates owe their leader, and the priests' lives are below the line. The pagan Greek principle of 'unjust orders need not be obeyed' is being enacted by Israelite Benjamites in their own scripture. Sometimes the most faithful loyalty an army can show its king is to refuse the order that would damn him.

And the king said to Doeg, Turn thou, and fall upon the priests. And Doeg the Edomite turned, and he fell upon the priests, and slew on that day fourscore and five persons that did wear a linen ephod. And Nob, the city of the priests, smote he with the edge of the sword, both men, and women, children, and sucklings, and oxen, and asses, and sheep, with the edge of the sword. Guidance: Saul turns to the one man in the room who will obey. 'Doeg the Edomite' — the narrator names his nationality each time as if to underline that an Edomite, not an Israelite, was willing to do this — and Doeg cuts down eighty-five priests in linen ephods. The linen ephod was the priestly vestment; Doeg is killing them in their ceremonial garments. The number — eighty-five — is staggering; a whole generation of priests gone in one afternoon. Then Doeg keeps going: he takes a detachment to Nob itself and exterminates the entire city. 'Men, and women, children, and sucklings, and oxen, and asses, and sheep.' The herem language of total destruction — the very language Saul had refused to apply to Agag and the Amalekites in chapter 15. Saul could not destroy the herem-cursed Amalek; he can destroy his own priests. This is the moral inversion of his reign distilled to a single act. The chapter is also a fulfillment of the curse pronounced on Eli's house in chapter 2 — 'I will raise me up a faithful priest… and I will cut off thine arm, and the arm of thy father's house, that there shall not be an old man in thine house' — Ahimelech is descended from Eli's line, and the cutting-off of that line is happening here, in a way no one could have foreseen, through the hand of a foreign herdsman acting under a rejected king. God's judgments sometimes work themselves out across generations through actors who think they are pursuing their own agendas.

And one of the sons of Ahimelech the son of Ahitub, named Abiathar, escaped, and fled after David. And Abiathar shewed David that Saul had slain the LORD's priests. Guidance: One survives. Abiathar, a son of Ahimelech, escapes the massacre and flees to David. The fulfillment of Eli's curse is not total; one descendant of Eli's house is preserved, and he will become David's priest for the next forty years, serving through the wilderness years and into David's reign. (Abiathar will eventually be exiled by Solomon in 1 Kings 2:26-27 as the final completion of Eli's curse — but that is decades away.) For now, Abiathar's escape means the high-priestly line of Israel continues with David, not with Saul. Abiathar arrives at David's camp and tells him what has happened. David hears the unbearable news: an entire city of priests has been slaughtered because of a meal of showbread and a borrowed sword.

And one of the sons of Ahimelech the son of Ahitub, named Abiathar, escaped, and fled after David. And Abiathar shewed David that Saul had slain the LORD's priests. Guidance: One survives. Abiathar, a son of Ahimelech, escapes the massacre and flees to David. The fulfillment of Eli's curse is not total; one descendant of Eli's house is preserved, and he will become David's priest for the next forty years, serving through the wilderness years and into David's reign. (Abiathar will eventually be exiled by Solomon in 1 Kings 2:26-27 as the final completion of Eli's curse — but that is decades away.) For now, Abiathar's escape means the high-priestly line of Israel continues with David, not with Saul. Abiathar arrives at David's camp and tells him what has happened. David hears the unbearable news: an entire city of priests has been slaughtered because of a meal of showbread and a borrowed sword.

And David said unto Abiathar, I knew it that day, when Doeg the Edomite was there, that he would surely tell Saul: I have occasioned the death of all the persons of thy father's house. Abide thou with me, fear not: for he that seeketh my life seeketh thy life: but with me thou shalt be in safeguard. Guidance: 'I have occasioned the death of all the persons of thy father's house.' One of the most candid confessions in scripture. David takes responsibility. He had seen Doeg at the sanctuary; he had calculated even then that Doeg would report; he had not warned Ahimelech. The lie he told to protect Ahimelech turned out to be the very thing that gave Doeg his selective testimony. David does not soften it: 'I have occasioned the death.' He owns it before Abiathar, the man who has every reason to hate him. Then comes the only thing David can offer: 'Abide thou with me, fear not.' He cannot raise the dead. He can cover the survivor. 'He that seeketh my life seeketh thy life: but with me thou shalt be in safeguard.' Abiathar gets a place at David's side and the high-priestly office in exile. The line that began with Eli's failed parenting in chapter 1 will continue, but not at Saul's court. The chapter has executed the judgment on Eli's house through Saul, and the LORD has rescued one priest into the David line so that the priesthood survives the king who tried to obliterate it. God's care for both judgment and mercy is, at its core, the same act. Even the chapter's darkest event leaves a thread that will run through David's reign as a witness that the LORD does not abandon His people, even when the king has.

And David said unto Abiathar, I knew it that day, when Doeg the Edomite was there, that he would surely tell Saul: I have occasioned the death of all the persons of thy father's house. Abide thou with me, fear not: for he that seeketh my life seeketh thy life: but with me thou shalt be in safeguard. Guidance: 'I have occasioned the death of all the persons of thy father's house.' One of the most candid confessions in scripture. David takes responsibility. He had seen Doeg at the sanctuary; he had calculated even then that Doeg would report; he had not warned Ahimelech. The lie he told to protect Ahimelech turned out to be the very thing that gave Doeg his selective testimony. David does not soften it: 'I have occasioned the death.' He owns it before Abiathar, the man who has every reason to hate him. Then comes the only thing David can offer: 'Abide thou with me, fear not.' He cannot raise the dead. He can cover the survivor. 'He that seeketh my life seeketh thy life: but with me thou shalt be in safeguard.' Abiathar gets a place at David's side and the high-priestly office in exile. The line that began with Eli's failed parenting in chapter 1 will continue, but not at Saul's court. The chapter has executed the judgment on Eli's house through Saul, and the LORD has rescued one priest into the David line so that the priesthood survives the king who tried to obliterate it. God's care for both judgment and mercy is, at its core, the same act. Even the chapter's darkest event leaves a thread that will run through David's reign as a witness that the LORD does not abandon His people, even when the king has.
